dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 en route for unconscious patient at 1230 14th Street.
Hi, I'm EMT Megan. What made you call 911 today?
We found her near-fainted and slouched in her wheelchair earlier this morning.
Let's assess her responsiveness. Can you tell me her name? What is today's date?
No verbal or motor response. She appears completely unresponsive.
Airway is patent, self-maintained, and clear of obstructions.
Confirming airway is clear, no signs of distress or stridor.
Let's assess her breathing. How deep and how fast is she breathing?
Her breathing rate is 14, shallow, and regular. Bilateral air entry present.
Heart rate is 122-fast but not erratic. Skin is warm and dry.
Pulse is 122-tachycardic. Initial SpO2 was 68% but improving.
End-tidal CO2 at 12 mmHg.
ECG shows sinus tachycardia with no immediate arrhythmia.
Pupils are unequal and reactive to light-left is 1 mm, right is 2 mm.
Could you share what the patient was doing or how she felt earlier today?
She mentioned shortness of breath and arm/shoulder pain today. No chest pain noted at the time.
Does she have a known medical history or chronic conditions?
She has mild asthma-no other conditions I'm aware of.
Her breathing has increased to 26 per minute now-shallow and rapid.
First reading was 0/0, but after re-check it was 145/102.
Blood glucose is 175 mg/dL.
SpO2 has improved to 92%.
Let's get IV access established now.
20-gauge IV in the arm. flushed and secured without complication.
Starting normal saline at KVO rate. A 1L bag is connected.
Taking a 12-lead ECG now due to tachycardia and findings.
12-lead shows ST elevation in I/aVL/V6, ST depression in III/aVF/V4, and T-wave inversion in V2/V3/V4-consistent with STEMI.
She's unconscious and showing signs of STEMI in anterior, inferior, lateral, and posterior territories-activating Protocol 3-15 now.
She remains unresponsive with occasional moans. IV secure and vital signs stable.
No new signs or symptoms. She remains shallow and breathing at 26.
Sending to [COMPANY_NAME] due to STEMI and altered mental status.
Notifying [COMPANY_NAME] of STEMI findings and requesting a trauma bed online.
Preparing to lift from wheelchair to cot. Using a sheet pull method with four attendants.
She's now positioned in the cot, strapped in x5, and rails raised.
She's loaded without complication. Lights and sirens on. Calling in STEMI alert progress.
The boyfriend is seated in the back with belt, and two RFD members are in rear with belts secured.
Vitals steady: 122/26, BP 145/102, SpO2 92, Glucose 175. 12-lead reconfirmed.
GCS remains 3. No neurological improvement.
She remains in stable condition for transport.
[COMPANY_NAME] has confirmed Trauma Bay 4 is prepared for arrival.
Comorbidities and anatomical details transmitted ahead. STEMI alert is in effect.
She moans but shows no motor or vocal response.
No interventions needed at this time. All medical protocols active and confirmed.
Full report given: vitals, interventions, ECG, STEMI status, and patient history. All documented.
Hospital staff is on alert. Patient status unchanged upon arrival.
Protocol 3-15 active for transport.
She initially reported shortness of breath and shoulder/arm pain-no chest noted.
RFD and patient guardian are monitored. No complications on transport team.
No apparent deformities or angulations. Mild tenderness in shoulder reported by bystander.
Skin remains dry and normal color, no rashes or scars observed.
Recheck vitals: 122, 26, 145/102, 92% SpO2, 175 mg/dL glucose, no changes to heart findings.
She is safely positioned on the backboard in the ambulance cot.
Activating STEMI alert for [COMPANY_NAME] upon arrival.
All details transmitted via cell phone ahead of arrival.
IV secure. All medications and tests completed prior to transport.
No indication of spinal trauma, so flexion is acceptable for safe transfer.
She is very stable during transport. Movement limited as expected.
Her comorbidities are noted, but the nature is not disclosed at this time.
Brief moans consistent with initial report. No worsening condition during transit.
No distension, masses, or tenderness observed upon abdominal inspection.
She is lying in full position. No limb movement outside standard range.
Urine color and flow not accessible for assessment in field.
No fresh blood or wounds identified en route. IV secure.
No abnormalities detected with heart rhythm expect initial findings.
No active bleeding or drainage noted. Tongue free to move.
Rechecking SpO2-still at 92%. No signs of hypoxia.
Patient received by receiving team with all relevant details shared.
All initial actions and signs explained to hospital staff on arrival.
Few people on scene. Only the boyfriend was with the patient.
Patient safely offloaded to Trauma Bay by sheet pull method.
No change in mental status. Fully transferred under safe conditions.
Full documentation handed over to receiving nurse including secondary assessments.
No evaluation for rigidity or pain on movement performed per field restrictions.
Clear lung fields on auscultation, no wheezes or crackles detected.
Her breathing rate still at 26. No signs of distress on visual monitoring.
Mild muscle tone uneven. No recent injuries observed.
STEMI findings fully described and STEMI alert shared prior to arrival.
Vertebral column appearing intact without visible crepitus or instability.
Bystander not in possession of patient's complete medical list.
Patient moved safely from ambulance to hospital bed using sheet pull with four person assist.
All medications unaltered at point of transfer. No additions documented.
Rechecked-CO2 12 mmHg, stable waveform on monitoring system.
Reassessed IV site-no infiltration. Running smoothly at KVO rate.
Full medical history shared with hospital staff, including STEMI details and vital signs.
No interventions needed further. Condition remains same on arrival.
No chest pain, no focal weakness, and no motor activity.
Noted earlier left arm/shoulder discomfort only, not chest-related.
Her anatomical background shared with hospital team on arrival.
She has known body structural anomalies, already communicated for care planning.
Blood pressure could not be effectively repeated due to patient anatomy challenges.
Adjustments noted ahead on communication channels for team awareness.
No changes in skin integrity, still dry and pink hue.
Report transmitted within expected window prior to arrival time.
Nobody else on scene with her, only significant other.
No additional witnesses or family members unavailable for interview.
No drugs at scene present. Bystander supportive for further care.
IV bag at correct pace, no signs of occlusion or breach.
No other interventions needed as of arrival. IV and vitals stable.
Patient was anxious prior to condition, but history is unknown to us.
No change noted post-initial treatment sequence. No additional concerns identified.
